patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record that causes it to abort recording when using Zstd compression, producing an undecompressable perf.data file. The patches address three issues: a 'Bad address' error, an output-buffer overrun, and a broken zstd-error fallback. The approach taken is to fix the bugs in the compressor function, specifically in how it handles multiple compressed records and buffer sizes.

review_comments.author: Namhyung Kim
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Namhyung Kim pointed out that the patch does not handle the case where the compressed data is larger than the output buffer size, which can lead to a buffer overflow.

Reviewer Namhyung Kim requested a rebase of the patch series onto the latest perf-tools-next branch, as it cannot apply anymore due to changes in that branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello,

On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 07:17:00AM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:

---

Can you please remove the blank line here?

Also, this series cannot apply anymore.  Please rebase onto the latest
perf-tools-next.

Thanks,
Namhyung

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents the perf record tool from storing raw data when ZSTD compression fails, which would lead to incorrect results and potential buffer overflows. Instead of copying the uncompressed input into the output buffer, it propagates the error. This fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that added support for Zstd streaming-based compression.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Zstd compression API used by perf record, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The fix involves passing the remaining space in the output buffer to a new function process_comp_header(), which checks if the record header and padding would fit before writing them. If not, it returns -1 to stop compressing. This prevents the API from writing past the end of the output buffer, fixing an underflow bug introduced by a previous patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the file when the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size, leading to mis-framed data that cannot be decompressed. The fix involves changing how the header is set for each record, ensuring that it accurately reflects the compressed data size and aligns properly with 8-byte boundaries. This change also reduces the maximum record size by the size of a u64 to prevent overflows in the header.size field.

review_comments.author: Namhyung Kim
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Namhyung Kim pointed out that the patch description does not clearly explain why the current fixup is wrong by construction and requested a more detailed explanation of the problem.

Reviewer Namhyung Kim requested a rebase of the patch series onto the latest perf-tools-next branch due to inapplicability and also asked for removal of an empty line in the code.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add build/runtime configs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new Kconfig option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make Syscall User Dispatch configurable. The feature allows a thread's system calls to be intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler outside an allowed IP address range.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Expressed confusion over the acronym 'IP' in the patch description, suggesting it should be clarified or rephrased for better understanding.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 09:58:58PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> +config SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH
> +	bool "Syscall User Dispatch"
> +	depends on GENERIC_ENTRY
> +	default y
> +	help
> +	  Syscall User Dispatch lets a thread have its own system calls outside
> +          an allowed IP address range to be intercepted and redirected to a
> +          userspace signal handler.

I was very confused when I read this.  IP expands to Internet Protocol
long before it gets to Instruction Pointer in my brain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new sysctl called kernel.syscall_user_dispatch to control whether userspace can arm syscall user dispatch via prctl or ptrace. The default value is 1, allowing arming of syscall user dispatch. Disabling it while a task has already armed it will not cause the task's dispatch to become inactive, but rather remain active until the user attempts to disable or re-enable it via prctl or ptrace. This prevents undefined behavior in userland when translating non-Linux syscalls to Linux syscalls.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new configurable feature, syscall_user_dispatch, which allows users to enable or disable the feature at both build and runtime. The feature is currently compiled in by default for all architectures that support CONFIG_GENERIC_SYSCALL, but this patch makes it optional.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Expressed confusion over the patch's explanation of IP expansion, suggesting a need for clearer documentation or explanation.
review_comments.raw_body:
